content.text: What Is a Simple Reflex Agent?
A simple reflex agent is the most basic form of intelligent agent. It selects actions by mapping the
current percept directly to an action

through a set of condition-action rules. It has no memory of past percepts and performs no lookahead. Every decision is a fresh lookup: if the current state matches a rule's condition, fire the corresponding action.

Despite this simplicity, reflex agents are widely used in production systems: traffic light controllers, thermostats, spam filters, network packet routers, and game AI for NPCs with predictable behaviour. Their simplicity is a feature — they are fast, predictable, and trivial to test.

attrs.alt: Simple reflex agent: percept goes through sensors, matched against condition-action rules, action sent to actuators, effect on environment
attrs.title: Simple Reflex Agent: Condition-Action Loop

content.text: The Condition-Action Rule
The core abstraction of a simple reflex agent is the condition-action rule (sometimes called a production rule):
IF
<condition is true of the current percept>
THEN
<execute this action>
Rules are evaluated in order. The first rule whose condition matches the current percept fires and the loop moves on. If no rule matches, a default action (often a no-op or a safe fallback) is taken.
Anatomy of a Simple Reflex Agent in Python
The thermostat is the canonical example: it reads the current temperature and turns heating or cooling on or off based purely on whether the current reading is above or below the target.

# Thermostat: a classic simple reflex agent
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ThermostatPercept:
    temperature: float  # Celsius
    target: float
    tolerance: float = 0.5

def thermostat_action(p: ThermostatPercept) -> str:
    if p.temperature < p.target - p.tolerance:
        return "heat_on"
    if p.temperature > p.target + p.tolerance:
        return "cool_on"
    return "idle"

# The agent has NO memory: it does not track how long the heater
# has been on or whether the temperature is rising or falling.
# Each call is completely independent.
print(thermostat_action(ThermostatPercept(temperature=18.0, target=20.0)))  # heat_on
print(thermostat_action(ThermostatPercept(temperature=20.3, target=20.0)))  # idle
print(thermostat_action(ThermostatPercept(temperature=22.0, target=20.0)))  # cool_on
Notice the critical characteristic:
the agent has no memory
. It does not know whether the temperature is rising or falling, how long the heater has been running, or what the temperature was five seconds ago. Each call to
thermostat_action
is completely independent.
A More Complex Example: Email Spam Filter
Even with multiple conditions, the structure is the same — a prioritised list of condition-action pairs. The spam filter below shows how you layer rules of increasing specificity:
The rule ordering matters.
More specific rules must come before more general ones.
If the 'newsletter' rule came before the 'known spam domain' rule, a spam email with an unsubscribe link would be misclassified.
Simple Reflex Agent vs. Model-Based Agent
Understanding where a simple reflex agent ends helps you know when to reach for a more powerful alternative:
Property
Simple Reflex Agent
Model-Based Agent
Memory
None
Tracks internal state
Handles partial observability
No
Yes
Planning
None
Possible via transition model
Latency
O(rules), sub-ms
Slightly higher
Testability
Very easy — pure function
Moderate — stateful
Use when
Env is fully observable
Env is partially observable
Testing Simple Reflex Agents
Simple reflex agents are
pure functions
— they have no side effects, no state, and no I/O. This makes them exceptionally easy to test with a parametrised table of (percept, expected action) pairs:

# Testing a simple reflex agent is straightforward:
# every test is just (percept -> expected action)
import pytest
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ThermostatPercept:
    temperature: float
    target: float
    tolerance: float = 0.5

def thermostat_action(p: ThermostatPercept) -> str:
    if p.temperature < p.target - p.tolerance:
        return "heat_on"
    if p.temperature > p.target + p.tolerance:
        return "cool_on"
    return "idle"

@pytest.mark.parametrize("temp,expected", [
    (18.0, "heat_on"),
    (19.6, "idle"),
    (20.0, "idle"),
    (20.4, "idle"),
    (22.0, "cool_on"),
])
def test_thermostat(temp, expected):
    p = ThermostatPercept(temperature=temp, target=20.0)
    assert thermostat_action(p) == expected

# No mocking, no async, no LLMs — purely table-driven tests.
# This is one of the biggest advantages of simple reflex agents.
This testing pattern — input/output tables with no setup or mocking — is one of the biggest practical advantages of keeping agents simple. The test suite documents every rule explicitly.
Where Simple Reflex Agents Fail
The agent's fundamental limitation is the assumption that the current percept contains everything needed to make the right decision. When that assumption breaks, the agent breaks with it.
Partial observability.
If a sensor fails or a signal is missing, the agent has no fallback. It will either take a default action (which may be wrong) or fail to match any rule.
Temporal patterns.

The agent cannot detect trends. A thermostat cannot tell the difference between a temperature that has been 18°C for an hour and one that dropped from 22°C ten seconds ago. Both get the same 'heat_on' action.
Multi-step goals.
If achieving a goal requires a sequence of actions that depend on each other, a reflex agent cannot coordinate them. Each action is chosen independently.
Environment state that is not immediately visible.
A robot navigating a maze needs to know where it has been. A reflex agent has no map.
When to Choose a Simple Reflex Agent
Choose a simple reflex agent when all of the following hold:
The environment is fully observable.
The current percept contains all information needed to choose the correct action.
The task is stateless.
The correct action does not depend on history — only on the current state.
The rule set is bounded.
The number of possible conditions is manageable and can be enumerated by a domain expert.
Latency and determinism matter.
You need sub-millisecond response times and a fully auditable, reproducible decision process.
If any of these conditions fail, consider a
model-based agent
(adds internal state), a
goal-based agent
(adds forward search), or a
utility-based agent
(adds a scoring function over outcomes).
Rule Priority and Conflict Resolution
When multiple rules could match a single percept, the agent must decide which action to take. This is the conflict resolution problem, and the choice of strategy changes behaviour significantly:
First-match (default).
Evaluate rules top-to-bottom; the first matching rule fires. The rule list is effectively a priority order. Most reflex agents use this strategy.
Last-match.
All matching rules are evaluated; the last match wins. Useful when override rules should be placed at the end of the list.
All-match.
Collect every matching action and pass the set to the environment. Used when multiple independent systems can act simultaneously (e.g. both sound an alarm AND send an SMS).
In practice, first-match with carefully ordered rules is the most common and most auditable strategy. The rule order is the specification — change the order and you change the agent.
Adding Priority Weights
Rather than relying purely on list position, you can assign explicit priority weights. This makes the specification more readable and separates the rule definition from the evaluation order:
The
explain
method is a useful addition to any reflex agent: it returns not just the action but the human-readable rule that fired it. This is critical for auditing production systems.
Simulating a Simple Reflex Agent Over Time
Even though a simple reflex agent has no memory, you can study its behaviour over a sequence of percepts by running a simulation. The simulation has the memory; the agent does not:

# Simulate a simple reflex agent over a sequence of percepts
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class Rule:
    condition: Callable[[dict], bool]
    action: str

def run_simulation(rules: list[Rule], percept_sequence: list[dict]) -> list[str]:
    actions = []
    for percept in percept_sequence:
        for rule in rules:
            if rule.condition(percept):
                actions.append(rule.action)
                break
        else:
            actions.append("no-op")
    return actions

# Robot vacuum: navigate a 1D corridor
vacuum_rules = [
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["dirt"] == "detected",          action="clean"),
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["bumper"] == "hit",             action="reverse_and_turn"),
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["battery"] < 20,                action="return_to_dock"),
    Rule(condition=lambda p: True,                             action="move_forward"),
]

percepts = [
    {"dirt": "none",     "bumper": "clear", "battery": 80},
    {"dirt": "detected", "bumper": "clear", "battery": 79},
    {"dirt": "none",     "bumper": "clear", "battery": 78},
    {"dirt": "none",     "bumper": "hit",   "battery": 77},
    {"dirt": "none",     "bumper": "clear", "battery": 15},
]

print(run_simulation(vacuum_rules, percepts))
# ["move_forward", "clean", "move_forward", "reverse_and_turn", "return_to_dock"]
This pattern —
agent as stateless function, simulation loop as state machine
— is useful for testing agent behaviour across many scenarios without modifying the agent itself. The agent's logic stays pure and testable; the simulation drives it through scenarios.
Building a Configurable Rule Engine

Hard-coding conditions as Python lambdas works in small systems but does not scale when business users need to tune the rules. A practical solution is to load rules from a configuration file — YAML, JSON, or a database — and interpret them at runtime:

# Load condition-action rules from a YAML config
# so non-engineers can tune the agent without touching code
import yaml
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Any

@dataclass
class Rule:
    field: str
    operator: str
    value: Any
    action: str

    def matches(self, percept: dict) -> bool:
        actual = percept.get(self.field)
        if self.operator == "eq":  return actual == self.value
        if self.operator == "gt":  return actual >  self.value
        if self.operator == "lt":  return actual <  self.value
        if self.operator == "gte": return actual >= self.value
        if self.operator == "lte": return actual <= self.value
        if self.operator == "in":  return actual in self.value
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown operator: {self.operator}")

RULES_YAML = """
rules:
  - field: temperature
    operator: lt
    value: 19.5
    action: heat_on
  - field: temperature
    operator: gt
    value: 20.5
    action: cool_on
  - field: co2_ppm
    operator: gt
    value: 1000
    action: open_vent
"""

def load_rules(yaml_str: str) -> list[Rule]:
    data = yaml.safe_load(yaml_str)
    return [Rule(**r) for r in data["rules"]]

class ConfigurableReflexAgent:
    def __init__(self, rules: list[Rule]):
        self.rules = rules

    def act(self, percept: dict) -> str:
        for rule in self.rules:
            if rule.matches(percept):
                return rule.action
        return "idle"

agent = ConfigurableReflexAgent(load_rules(RULES_YAML))
print(agent.act({"temperature": 18.0, "co2_ppm": 400}))   # heat_on
print(agent.act({"temperature": 20.0, "co2_ppm": 1200}))  # open_vent
This approach separates
rule definition (configuration)
from
rule evaluation (code)
. A domain expert can add a new temperature threshold without touching Python. The agent's logic remains a pure
act(percept) -> action
function; only the data it consults changes.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Catch-all rule in the wrong position.
A rule whose condition is always true (like
lambda p: True
) must be the last rule in the list. Placed first, it shadows all other rules and the agent always takes the default action.
Overlapping conditions with ambiguous priority.
When two rules can both match the same percept, the ordering determines the outcome silently. Document the intended priority in code comments or use explicit priority weights.
Side effects inside conditions.
Conditions should be pure read-only checks. If a condition function modifies state or has I/O side effects, it will fire for every percept evaluated before its match, which is usually unintended.
Testing only the happy path.
Always include percepts that should match no rule (testing the default action) and percepts designed to expose ordering bugs.

# Common mistake: condition that is always True placed too early
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

@dataclass
class Rule:
    condition: Callable[[dict], bool]
    action: str

# BAD: catch-all rule at position 0 shadows everything below
bad_rules = [
    Rule(condition=lambda p: True,              action="no-op"),  # always fires!
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["dirt"],         action="clean"),  # never reached
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["bumper_hit"],   action="reverse"),# never reached
]

# GOOD: catch-all at the end as a safe default
good_rules = [
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["dirt"],         action="clean"),
    Rule(condition=lambda p: p["bumper_hit"],   action="reverse"),
    Rule(condition=lambda p: True,              action="no-op"),  # safe default
]

# Also watch for: overlapping conditions with no clear priority
# Solution: add an explicit priority field or enforce non-overlapping conditions
def validate_rules(rules: list[Rule], test_percepts: list[dict]) -> None:
    for percept in test_percepts:
        matches = [r for r in rules if r.condition(percept)]
        if len(matches) > 1:
            actions = [r.action for r in matches]
            print(f"Warning: {len(matches)} rules match {percept}: {actions}")
Summary
A simple reflex agent maps percepts to actions through condition-action rules; it has no memory or planning.
Rules are evaluated in order; the first matching condition fires its action. Rule ordering is part of the agent's specification.
The pattern works well for thermostats, traffic controllers, spam filters, and any fully observable, stateless task.
The key limitation is the requirement for full observability — if the current percept is incomplete, the agent cannot compensate with remembered context.
Simple reflex agents are pure functions and therefore trivially testable with input/output tables.
